Absurdism in The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy and Alice's Adventures in Wonderland

body: International Baccalaureate Extended Essay — Group 1: English (Category 1). Session: May 2018.
Research question: What is the role of absurdism in Lewis Carroll’s Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland and Douglas Adams’ The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy?

sections.heading: I. Introduction
body: Since their publications in 1865 and 1979 respectively, Lewis Carroll’s Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland (AAIW) and Douglas Adams’ The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy (THGTTG) have solely been categorized in the fantasy genre. While THGTTG pursues the misadventures of Arthur Dent, who travels to space and explores the vast array of galaxies and planets with an unexpected group of friends, AAIW revolves around Alice who enters a fantastic realm populated by anthropomorphic creatures. Both novels encompass a fictional world and contain fantastical elements, but they also express the tumultuous experiences and mental anguish of the protagonists who attempt to impose meaning on an irrational world. Thus, I believe that the novels offer profound insights on absurdism through the plot, characters, and setting. My research question is, “What is the role of absurdism in The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy and Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland?” Identified most often for their fantasy elements, it seems relevant to investigate why the novels encapsulate intricate philosophical ideas and how these ideas impact the readers.

To answer the research question, commentaries and literary theories will be called upon. Michael Y. Bennett’s The Cambridge Introduction to Theatre and Literature of the Absurd and Albert Camus’ The Myth of Sisyphus will be used as guiding materials to identify the objective and characteristics of absurdist literature. These sources were chosen as they were published from a reliable establishment, increasing their authenticity. The purpose of absurdism in THGTTG and AAIW specifically will be evaluated using research, analysis and personal interpretation. I will first examine the lack of causal relationships in AAIW and THGTTG and its relevance. Then, I will explore how the peculiar characterization of central characters sheds light on absurdism. Lastly, I will discuss how Adams and Carroll manipulate the setting to highlight the meaninglessness of life.

sections.heading: II. Background of Absurdism
body: Gaining popularity in the 1950s, absurdist philosophy was a response to World War II. (Bennett) With the loss of a stable social structure, religious ideologies, and nationalism, people adopted an existential point of view. What absurdist literature sought to do was capture the disconnect between man and the world. (Camus) Other philosophers identified that the world itself was irrational. This paved the way for the existentialist and absurdist philosophy. While absurdity relates to the divorce between man and his life, existentialism investigates the purposelessness of life. (Esslin) Together, the two philosophies explore the lack of meaning in the Universe.

Michael Y. Bennett, author of The Cambridge Introduction to Theatre and Literature of the Absurd, has identified three common threads in absurdist literature. Firstly, absurdist authors experiment with language to illuminate its ambiguity and “how the lack of linguistic precision could lead to miscommunication and confusion.” (Bennett 18). Secondly, absurdist literature follows a non-Aristotelian plot, often taking on the structure of a parable - a didactic story. Readers are forced to make sense of the contradictions, extended metaphors, clashing viewpoints, and paradoxes that accompany the parable structure. This sheds light on how hard it is to discern the meaning of life. Lastly, the literary works are set in strange situations that are surreal and ridiculous. This again imparts a feeling of absurdity as readers are forced to comprehend the irrational and nonsensical world. Altogether, absurdist literature focuses on recounting the existential school of thought - the lack of meaning in the world.

sections.heading: III. Plot
body: While most novels follow an Aristotelian plot, absurdist literature detracts from the typical literary conventions. Instead, they take on the structure of a parable, which lends a sense of absurdity to the novel. This is seen in both THGTTG and AAIW.

A non-linear narrative is a characteristic of THGTTG. A good example of this is when Arthur Dent accidentally provokes an interstellar battle between two extraterrestrial races, the G’Gugvuntt and Vl’hurgs, when he states, “I seem to be having tremendous difficulty with my lifestyle.” (Adams 195) In the Vl’hurgs tongue, this phrase is “the most dreadful insult imaginable.” (196) It spawns a horrible war between the two species and lasts for a thousand years. Eventually, the G’Gugvuntt and the Vl’hurgs realize that their battle was the result of miscommunication and that the phrase was from another Galaxy. So, they ally forces and take revenge on the first planet they encounter - Earth. Again, due to an illogical miscalculation, their entire battle fleet is “accidently swallowed by a small dog.” (196) Through this non sequitur, Adams emphasizes how the characters are powerless to the interplay of cause and effect. The coincidence of the planet being Earth and the dog swallowing the fleet to save humankind is hyperbolic and humorous, as readers wouldn’t expect a dog to be the solution of an intergalactic war. This again highlights that there are no coincidences and history is but a “complex interplay of cause and effect.” (196) Adams also mentions that the events take place “over thousands of years” (196) but explains the events in a page, which is an understatement. The contrast trivializes the cause-effect model, as the causal relationships are exaggerated and ludicrous. Moreover, readers are puzzled by the fragmented anecdotes’ relevance in the context of the larger plot, but it serves to add chaos to the story. In doing so, Adams points out that the world is so meaningless and senseless that there is no way to deal with it rationally. The aim of the non sequitur is to dispel readers’ beliefs that there is a pattern in history and that there is meaning to actions, underscoring the absurd nature of life.

Lewis Carroll also adds meaningless chaos to AAIW to deliver absurdism. This is seen when the Queen of Hearts and the jury convene to find out who stole the Queen’s tarts. A trial should be serious and formal, however, the one in the novel is the opposite. Alice begins to grow and her skirt accidentally tips over the jury box. The jurors are “sprawling about” and Alice tries to put them back “as quickly as she could.” (Carroll 103) Meanwhile, the Lizard (Alice’s friend) waves “its tail about in a melancholy way” (103) as it is upside down in the jury box. The use of numerous action words suggests that there is a lot of movement and animation at the Court of Justice. However, trials are known to be slow, and jurors have to pay close attention to what the victims and suspects have to say. By writing about an impractical and irrational trial, Carroll satirizes its monotonous nature. The plot takes a new turn when the Queen says, “Sentence first - verdict afterwards.” (107) She essentially states that all the suspects should be executed and then the verdict should be made. To do this would be quite illogical as innocent suspects would be punished along with the perpetrator. Thus, the unconventionality of the trial highlights the nonsensical nature of AAIW and the inutile actions of Alice knocking the jury box add to the meaningless chaos.

This is further demonstrated when the Queen of Hearts and Alice play a game of croquet. However, the game has a nonsensical twist – there are no rules, anyone can do whatever they want to, and the game uses animals and soldiers as apparatus. The lack of rules emphasizes that there is no winner just like the Brockian Ultra Cricket game from THGTTG, where people randomly hit each other and run away. In croquet, animals are used as props and soldiers blatantly “walk off to other parts of the ground,” (74) Alice gets frustrated and eventually gives up. This mimics our search for life in a meaningless and uncompromising world. The props that do whatever they want make it hard for Alice to achieve anything. Through Alice’s actions, Carroll illustrates his belief on how humans try to impose meaning on a random, arbitrary, and meaningless world. Carroll shows that type of thinking can be dangerous, especially since life is constantly evolving, just like the props always getting up and ruining the game for Alice. Moreover, Alice is in a state of absurdity as she tries to understand the irrational world around her but fails to do so. Alice’s mental anguish drives her to quit the game. Through this, Carroll demonstrates how humans’ propensity to find meaning in an irrational world could lead to insanity. As these ideas are discussed in the context of a game, it suggests that there is a prize for finding the meaning of life. But when there is no winner because the game loses its structure in mindless chaos, readers understand that it is futile to search for meaning, as there is no right answer like there is no winner.

Following a nonlinear plot, AAIW and THGTTG impart a feeling of absurdity. In doing so, they shed light on life’s trivial and mundane nature. They also express the difficulties of finding life’s meaning in a nonsensical world.

sections.heading: IV. Characterization
body: While most absurdist literature might not focus on the development of characters, characters play a huge role in lending a sense of absurdity to AAIW and THGTTG. With an abundance of them fleeting through the novels, they contribute to the nonsensical nature of the plot. Using Bennett’s four characteristics of absurdist literature as guiding material is appropriate; however, AAIW and THGTTG aren’t known for their philosophical viewpoints, so it is safe to assume that they might not adhere to all the conventions of absurdist literature. Thus, I will investigate the characters in AAIW and THGTTG and how they contribute to existentialism.

The President of the Imperial Galactic Government, Zaphod Beeblebrox, is an apt example of an absurd character. He cauterizes all the synapses in his brain and electronically traumatizes the central parts of the cerebellum without knowing it. (Adams 146) He only realizes that he is responsible for this when he conducts encephalographic tests and finds the ZB initials burned in his brain. Unaware of why he did this to himself, he contemplates, “Whatever happened to my mind, I did it… And I wasn’t to know anything about it myself.” (191) Zaphod would never be able to identify who did it because he erased the memories that contained this information. This scheme resembles humans’ search for meaning and inherent purposelessness. By revealing Zaphod’s history during the climax of the play, readers become keen to know why Zaphod did this to himself but they are denied the closure, similar to life’s meaning. Adams does throw the readers a bone by including one of the possible explanations for Zaphod’s actions: Zaphod hypothesizes that he did this to himself in order to fulfill his promise to Yooden, the former president. However, this explanation is vague and disappointing because it isn’t congruous with Zaphod’s actions: Zaphod erases half his memories and puts himself through a lot of pain just to fulfill his promise to Yooden, but doesn’t even remember why he promised to steal the Heart of Gold spaceship because of his cauterized brain. This explanation is illogical and unsatisfactory. Through this, Adams hints that the answer to the meaning of life will also be disappointing. Thus, Zaphod’s actions add to the novel’s absurdism.

Marvin the Robot from THGTTG also demonstrates the traits of an absurd character. He is “a new generation of Sirius Cybernetics Corporation robots and computers with the new GPP [Genuine People Personalities] features.” (94) In 1979, when THGTTG was published, robots were believed to be unintelligent machines that would simply serve to relieve humans of menial tasks. However, science fiction novels portrayed robots as intelligent forms of life that had the capacity to take over the world. (Erkenbrecher) Adams satirizes both these ideas by combining them in the most unexpected way. While Marvin has the intellectual capabilities and outsmarts all his companions, he is subjected to menial tasks like bringing Arthur and Ford to the bridge when they materialize on the Heart of Gold. His frustration is illustrated when he says “Here I am, brain the size of a planet and they ask me to take you down to the bridge.” (95) The juxtaposition between his intellectual superiority and banal tasks coupled with an underlying sarcastic tone emphasizes his resentful yet introspective nature, characteristics that aren’t attributed to robots. In fact, readers expect robots to be emotionless, but Marvin feels a spectrum of feelings, including terminal depression. This intense display of emotion in lifeless and mechanical inanimate objects like robots makes his characterization all the more absurd, humorous, and peculiar. Furthermore, humans take pride in their ability to feel. They believe that this particular quality enables them to be the smartest species on Earth. The fact that robots, which are manmade and artificial, can feel with such intensity suggests that humans aren’t as superior as they believe to be. With this new revelation, readers are in anguish. They realize that their emotions might not be as legitimate and natural, forcing them to question the legitimacy of all their experiences. This relates back to the existentialist philosophy – if emotions can be rendered, then they aren’t authentic, which means that they don’t hold any real meaning. This also reiterates the point that even if one is interested in finding a purpose, the world unavoidably forces him/her to succumb to the purposelessness of life. So, even though Marvin can achieve tremendous feats, he is subjected to useless menial tasks that don’t add any value to his life. It proves that human purposelessness is a condition that every human is entitled to and cannot get rid of.

The characterization of the Hatter in AAIW also imparts a sentiment of absurdity. The Hatter is introduced in A Mad Tea Party (Carroll 60), which is a parody of the then-prevailing iconic Victorian tea parties. Carroll’s tea party lacks etiquette, structure and the mannerisms of the traditional Victorian tea party (Mouten). During the tea party, Hatter asks Alice a riddle, ‘Why is a raven like a writing-desk?’ (60) without knowing the answer himself. Just like Alice, readers attempt to answer the riddle by thinking of similarities between a raven and a writing desk, but readers are lost. This is similar to when humans attempt to find meaning in a meaningless world. The riddle essentially symbolizes our desire to learn more about our life. However, the irrational question suggests that humans might be asking the wrong questions or maybe that humans’ questions are so illogical in the context of the world that they don’t have an answer. Moreover, the Hatter can also be considered as a wise fool. A wise fool characteristically rejects the norms of the culture, and this makes him appear foolish. (Keen) The Hatter does this by asking a riddle without any answer. Riddles always have an answer, so the Hatter is essentially breaking rules. Interestingly, this very act forces readers to deliberate over the riddle. The ludicrous character provokes such insightful thought through the oxymoron and forces readers to ponder over the meaning of life. The story progresses as Alice tells the Hatter that he “might do something better with the time… then wasting it in asking riddles that have no answers.” (Carroll 63) Through Alice’s response, Carroll implicitly mocks humans’ innate desire to find the meaning of life and answers the question – there is no meaning to life.

The characters in AAIW and THGTTG play a huge role in the way that absurdist ideas are conveyed to the readers. Even though the characters aren’t humans, they ironically explicate the absurdity of human condition through their speech and actions like the mental anguish due to the purposelessness of life.

sections.heading: V. Setting
body: AAIW and THGTTG have unusual settings that are intermittently juxtaposed with ordinary existing surroundings, revealing more about humans’ current predicaments in a humorous and absurd way. These bizarre and unnatural settings force readers to reflect inwards and allow “for explicit satirical criticism to be applied on the status quo of human existence.” (Erkenbrecher) Nonsensical settings are one of Bennett’s four common threads of absurdist literature, further driving home the purpose of absurdism and existentialism in the respective novels.

The impact of a bizarre setting is illustrated in THGTTG when Phouchg and Loonquawl - members of the hyperintelligent pandimensional race - demand the answer to “the great Question of Life, and the Universe and Everything,” (Adams 179) after an arduous seven and a half million years of waiting. The quick exchange of dialogues, analogous with stichomythia, builds the tension and anticipation for the answer. But, Deep Thought’s answer, the number 42, is anticlimactic and disappointing because it doesn’t explicitly respond to the meaning of life. Moreover, the non sequitur answer isn’t followed by context or an explanation, and so Deep Thought’s response trivializes the importance of the question. As elaborated in the Dictionary of Literary Biography, the meaninglessness of 42 “demonstrates that surface is everything and nothing has any meaningful substance. All the so-called absolute answers… turn out to be shams or irritatingly banal.” (Harris-Fain 5) Adams deliberately uses an inappropriate and random answer to emphasize his belief that there is no inherent meaning of human existence. By portraying humans’ innate desire to find the answer to life through the characterization of the hyperintelligent pandimensional race and denying a satisfying closure, Adams mimics our reality and sheds light on the verisimilitude nature of life. The story progresses as Deep Thought warns the hyperintelligent species that they have the right answer but the wrong question, and the hyperintelligent species eagerly begin their search for the right question. The repetition of a similar narrative mitigates the magnitude of and even characteristically pokes fun at humans’ desire to find the meaning to life. Had Adams explored using characters that were humans, the novel might not have been as impactful. Thus, the setting allows him to explore these ideas unhindered.

This is also revealed when Arthur and his alien best friend Ford Prefect are captured by the Vogons, the ugliest and meanest species in the Universe. Prostetnic Vogon Jeltz, the commander of the Vogon Constructor fleet spaceship, throws them into space in order to get rid of them. Fortunately, the Heart of Gold spaceship rescues both of them due to the Infinite Improbability Drive. While this is logical in the context of the novel – the function of the Infinite Improbability Drive is to produce the least probable event – this isn’t a viable solution in real life. This is because the Heart of Gold spaceship is five hundred thousand years away from the Vogon Constructor Fleet. Travelling at the speed of light (the fastest speed known to man), it would take five years for the spaceship to cross this distance and rescue Ford and Arthur. However, it reaches within 29 seconds. The spaceship inexplicably defies all laws of science, making it ludicrous and therefore absurd. This is reiterated when The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy states, “the chances of getting picked up by another ship… are two to the power of two hundred and seventy-six thousand seven hundred and nine to one against.” (Adam 77) As this number is neither accessible nor imaginable, the hyperbole demonstrates that the situation is unattainable other than in absurd and unrealistic situations. Moreover, the Infinite Improbability Drive influences the plot in a random manner, acting as a deus ex machina. While this technique is criticized for its groundlessness and unreasonableness, Adams uses it for that very purpose. With the help of the Infinite Improbability Drive, Adams seamlessly resolves the difficult situation and highlights the inherent deficiencies of plot and life. The fact that Adams creates an artful device to save his protagonists from an insolvable situation goes to show that anything can happen. As the plot is a reflection of life, this idea demonstrates that life is unpredictable and random as well. Adams subtly explicates that life cannot be confined to one meaning. Again, the setting of the novel, an irrational and ludicrous Universe, allows him to incorporate deus ex machina to resolve the plot. Writing the novel in a rational world with insane characters would make it harder for readers to decipher the philosophical thoughts.

Carroll also examines the meaninglessness of life by reducing language to its semantics, which is one of the characteristics of absurdist literature identified by Bennett. The best example of this is seen in the Mock Turtle and the Gryphon’s speech. They continuously exploit and denounce the syllogistic structure of language, emptying their speech of any meaning. (Wegener) This is seen when Alice and the two discuss their school lives and she asks them how long their lessons are to which the Mock Turtle replies that they decrease by an hour every day. The Gryphon clarifies “that’s the reason they’re called lessons… because they lessen from day to day.” (Carroll 85) While this pun might seem trivial and humorous, it exemplifies the extent to which double meaning and ambiguity can affect one’s reality. The animals interpret lesson as an extension of the word lessen, terms that are only differentiated by one letter, and that becomes their truth and interpretation of the world. This proves that language is an important way of acquiring and transferring knowledge, and so if it has substantial flaws, then it also affects our perception of reality. Interestingly, these types of puns and miscommunications pepper the novel and make it hard for the reader to truly understand where the plot is going.

Carroll also manipulates the fundamental pillars of our reality, size and time, by making these quantities relative to each character. As a result, there aren’t any physical quantities left to give reality an objective truth. This is seen when Time stops for the Hatter as he makes a grievous mistake of boring the Queen of Hearts, to which she replies, “He’s murdering the time… Off with his head!” (64) Again, there is a miscommunication of ideas: what the Queen means is that the Hatter is wasting time, but Time, which is personified, believes that the Hatter is threatening him and so he stops “working” for the Hatter. Taking the pun literally leads to dire consequences. In this way, characters’ sizes keep fluctuating. For example, every time Alice eats something, she grows or shrinks relative to others. Thus, this depicts that characters have no control over their own reality, making it difficult to set one universal truth. This proves yet again that there is no objectivity to life. The world is so chaotic and temperamental that it is hard to impose meaning on it. Creating a bizarre setting allows Carroll to explore these philosophical elements.

Creating fantasy worlds paradoxically guides readers to better understand the reality that they are trying to evade. Thus, Adams and Carroll deliberately set their novels in the Wonderland and the Universe to comment on humans’ propensity to mold meaning from the madness and the purposelessness of life.

sections.heading: VI. Conclusion
body: Although The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy and Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland broadly belong to the fantasy genre, both novels express intricate philosophical ideas. Adams and Carroll suggest that life is meaningless and has no inherent value through the plot, characterization, and setting. Both the novels’ plots lack causal relationships, exemplifying the inherent deficiencies in life. Furthermore, the characterizations of Zaphod Beeblebrox, Marvin the Robot, and the Hatter shed light on absurdism. Zaphod, being the President of the Imperial Galactic Government, is atypically nonsensical and ludicrous. Marvin the Robot has terminal depression, and his intense display of emotions is paradoxical to his intelligence. With his GPP features, he is unable to work to his full potential. The Hatter is an archetype of a wise fool, and he forces readers to question their pre-existing notions of life’s meaning. Lastly, the settings of both novels are peculiar and irrational. Neither physical laws nor rules govern the fictional worlds, demonstrating life’s meaninglessness. The role of absurdism in AAIW and THGTTG is to shed light on the senselessness of life. Adams and Carroll attempt to prove that life doesn’t have a rigid meaning and a regimented purpose, rather it is open to interpretation. The didactic message is that it is pointless to impose meaning on a chaotic and irrational world.
